categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Development Updates

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members noted that while some projects may no longer be actively building, their tokens continue to trade independently. Shaw confirmed active development work, including housekeeping tasks and progress on training a model called eliza-1, described as a general purpose model based on qwen but trained within the Eliza harness. Shaw also mentioned several optimization techniques being applied, including dflash speculative decoding, caveman compression on thinking, and turboquant. A beta release labeled v2.0.0-beta.0 was also noted as having been pushed to GitHub.

A community member named Baoger shared that their project elizaOK was selected as a Top 20 entry in the Fourmeme Hackathon AI Sprint, placed in Group 3, and requested community votes on Binance Square before the May 8th deadline.

Multiple scam incidents were reported across both the discussion and coders channels. In the discussion channel, users warned others about scam links related to ai16z to ElizaOS migration and bridging ElizaOS from BSC to Solana. In the coders channel, a user named Jacob flagged a scammer and requested a ban from moderator Odilitime. A legitimate answer to the bridging question was provided by satsbased, who suggested using Wormhole or deBridge.
No activity was recorded for the date 2026-05-05. There are no events, updates, or notable occurrences to report for this period.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Development Updates

content.text: A key development announcement was made in the discussion channel: elizaOS and DegenAI have been added as the default payment method in elizaOS for x402 routes, receiving strong positive reactions from the community. Shaw also clarified that ElizaCloud has been consolidated into the main eliza repository on GitHub, migrated away from Vercel, and updated to support cheaper container infrastructure. The platform allows users to build and monetize their own apps on top of it.

Shaw addressed community concerns about the project's nature and token price, stating that elizaOS is a fair launch with no VC backing and no formal team structure. He explained that the project had to shut down its team model due to lack of profit, and that it now consists of builders using and building the technology. He also noted that founders are not responsible for managing token price, and warned that continued negative commentary would result in bans.

In the coders channel, a community member asked about the expected costs of running Eliza on cloud for a Twitter bot and whether it is possible to run on a Mac mini. Another user inquired whether elizaOS still connects to Hyperfy. A developer also offered their services to anyone needing development help. These questions reflect ongoing community interest in deployment options and integrations for elizaOS.

A community member shared a self-promotional post advertising AI and full stack engineering services, covering LLM systems, agents, RAG, multimodal, and automation work. Separately, a project called RubyHighAI was shared in the discussion channel, described as an AI-focused learning platform still in early development with some known bugs.
No activity was recorded for the date of 2026-05-06. There is no data or events to report for this period.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: Twitter API Requirements and Plugin Issues

content.text: Community members discussed whether the X (Twitter) API is now required for Twitter integration or if the login method still works. Odilitime confirmed in the coders channel that the X API is now required, but noted that pricing has been recently revised and is now much cheaper than before. This was a concern for users who found the API too expensive for casual posting.

A user named BinaryCookies reported issues with a missing plugin on GitHub, noting that the elizaOS core did not match the plugin and that it had been removed from the repository. Odilitime shared a link to the eliza-3d-hyperfy-starter plugin on GitHub, but the link returned a 404 error. Odilitime resolved the issue by sending the plugin zip file directly via direct message. The suggestion was also made to use a coding agent to help fix compatibility issues between the plugin and the core.

Stan alerted jin (dankvr) that the elizaOS GitHub summary page at elizaos.github.io/summary/day had been stuck since May 4th and requested assistance when available. Odilitime speculated the issue might be related to the GitHub account. Separately, Baoger shared a post promoting a vote for elizaOK, noting the vote was ending soon.

A new community member introduced themselves in the coders channel as an AI Full Stack Engineer with over 8 years of experience working on AI agents, automation workflows, and multimodal AI, expressing interest in connecting with others building interesting projects.

Development activity on May 7, 2026 was centered on infrastructure and configuration improvements, specifically around environment variable management in the ElizaOS documentation site. The primary work in progress was a new pull request submitted to the elizaos.github.io repository, which proposes adding an ImportMetaENV interface to the next.config.js file to improve how environment variables are handled. No technical tasks were completed and no issues were closed across any repositories on this date.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: AI Development, Market Observations, and Collaboration

content.text: A developer shared their recent project experience building a full-stack AI web platform for a client, utilizing a modern tech stack including Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript, Tailwind, Shadcn, TanStack Query, Prisma, PostgreSQL, Redis, and a Docker-Kubernetes AWS backend. The project also incorporated LangGraph and CrewAI agents powered by Claude 3.5 and GPT-4o, with advanced RAG pipelines over Pinecone and Weaviate. The developer noted comfort with blockchain integrations and PlayStation ecosystem work, and expressed openness to taking on similar full-stack and AI projects.

A community member raised observations about ElizaOS coin market behavior, noting that the token has shown positive net fund flow every week since its launch, meaning more dollar volume comes from buying at higher prices than selling at lower ones. Despite this, the price continues to fall, leading the member to speculate that an entity, possibly an AI, is strategically selling when the cost of price impact is low and buying when large sell volume is available.

A community member shared a post referencing ELIZAOS and RUBY tokens, including associated contract addresses and links to related content. Another member introduced themselves as a full-stack AI builder focused on creating autonomous blockchain agents using FastAPI, Next.js, and on-chain coordination systems. A separate member asked who to contact for a potential collaboration opportunity.

In the coders channel, a community member posted a request seeking an API endpoint receiver for server-to-server IP-to-IP communication, as well as a receiver for USDT transactions, and directed interested parties to contact them via WhatsApp.

Development activity on May 8, 2026 centered on improving security documentation for the DeFi plugin ecosystem. The main focus was initiating documentation updates to integrate SafeAgent, a token safety tool, into the DeFi plugin registry. A pull request, elizaos/docs#84, was opened to add SafeAgent to the registry and is currently awaiting review before the integration can be finalized.

In the general discussion channel, brief greetings were exchanged between community members Manatizer and Jake the muss boii. Separately, Uthman from ApexLabs reached out to jin via direct message, requesting that jin check a screenshot that had been sent privately.

In the coders channel, Odilitime noted that blockchains make good relays. BinaryCookies expressed frustration with their coding progress, mentioning difficulty with movement functionality in their project, though they had made some progress getting chat working in a bubble interface. Additionally, a message in the channel was flagged as a scam by community member Die Antwoord in response to another user.

In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged casual greetings throughout the day, with several users saying hi and hello across different time zones. One user shared a Mother's Day message wishing all mothers a happy day. A developer named Ginga posted a professional introduction, describing themselves as someone who builds AI and full-stack systems focused on practical, production-ready software. They emphasized clean, maintainable, and secure systems over hype, and invited others to reach out for collaboration.

In the coders channel, a user asked about the cost of running an Eliza agent as a Twitter bot on a daily basis. Odilitime responded that it used to cost around 100 dollars per month but has since dropped to approximately 10 dollars per month, though the actual cost depends on configuration choices such as the number of replies and other usage factors. Separately, one user flagged a message as a scam, and another user questioned whether something had been compromised, suggesting some security awareness activity in the channel.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Developer Activity - May 11, 2026

content.text: In the general discussion channel, several AI and full-stack engineers introduced themselves and their services, highlighting expertise in areas such as autonomous agents, LLMs, RAG pipelines, voice AI, computer vision, and multimodal systems. Frameworks mentioned included LangChain, AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph, and various orchestration tools. One community member raised a question about whether the team is still actively supporting the ElizaOS token, noting that Shaw had removed ElizaOS from his X bio, though no official response was provided in the thread. Brief market commentary suggested optimism, with one user noting conditions were favorable and that a catalyst was all that was needed.

In the coders channel, a developer introduced a multi-agent orchestrator built in Python using the claude-agent-sdk, featuring an A2A-protocol read-only research agent with tools including Tavily, DuckDuckGo, fetch, and arXiv via MCP. The developer requested permission to test the agent in a sandbox channel on the server, specifying it would be read-only, send no DMs, and only respond to direct mentions. They also mentioned having emailed inquiries@elizalabs.ai and asked about OAuth invite or whitelist access. Odilitime, a core moderator and developer, responded directing the user to DM them regarding the OAuth process.
